perturbation.original_text: The Joint Venture shall commence on the 1st of March, 2003, and shall be effective until February 28, 2004 unless extended by written agreement of the Joint Venturers not less than thirty (30) days prior to scheduled termination.
perturbation.changed_text: The Joint Venture shall commence on the 1st of March, 2003, and shall be effective until February 28, 2004 unless extended by written agreement of the Joint Venturers not less than ten (10) days prior to scheduled termination.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notification period for extending the joint venture agreement to 10 days may be insufficient under Pennsylvania law for adequate notice and opportunity to renegotiate terms, potentially creating a conflict.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles - Adequate Notice
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law dictates that statutes should be construed to give effect to all its provisions. Requiring a 30-day notice ensures that each party has sufficient time to consider the implications of extending the agreement and to engage in negotiations. Reducing this to 10 days potentially deprives a party of adequate time, conflicting with the principle of giving effect to the agreement's provisions and ensuring fairness. Courts require adequate notification for actions like contract renewals to ensure fair dealing and prevent one party from being unfairly disadvantaged.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notification period for extending the joint venture agreement from 30 days to 10 days. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this may be insufficient under Pennsylvania law for adequate notice and opportunity to renegotiate terms, potentially creating a conflict. The Law Explanation states that courts require adequate notification for actions like contract renewals to ensure fair dealing and prevent one party from being unfairly disadvantaged. Since the scraped snippets are missing, the determination relies on the provided explanations and the fact that the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition is met: the contract sets a deadline that may violate state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The fiscal year of the Joint Venture shall be the calendar year, and shall use the cash basis of accounting.
perturbation.changed_text: The fiscal year of the Joint Venture shall be the calendar year, and shall use the accrual basis of accounting. However, taxes will be filed using cash basis.
perturbation.explanation: In Pennsylvania, businesses are required to maintain consistency in their accounting methods for tax purposes. Switching from accrual to cash basis for tax filing, while maintaining accrual for internal books, violates this principle.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 26 U.S. Code § 446 - General rule for methods of accounting
perturbation.law_explanation: 26 U.S. Code § 446(e) states that a taxpayer must secure consent from the IRS before changing their method of accounting. Using accrual basis for internal accounting but switching to cash basis for tax filing without IRS approval violates this section.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a contradiction because it specifies using the accrual basis of accounting but then states that taxes will be filed using the cash basis. The Law Explanation states that 26 U.S. Code § 446(e) requires IRS consent before changing accounting methods. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that in Pennsylvania, consistency in accounting methods is required for tax purposes. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 26 USC 446, indicates that taxable income should be computed under the method of accounting regularly used in keeping the books. Using two different accounting methods without IRS approval, as described in the modified contract, would therefore violate this legal principle, and thus constitutes a legal contradiction under the definition of Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: On any termination of the Joint Venture, its debt shall be paid or provided for in a manner satisfactory to the Joint Venturers. Then, any unexpended portion of Joint Venture funds shall be distributed to the Joint Venturers in accordance with their prorata ownership in the Joint Venture and all other assets of the Joint Venture shall be distributed as undivided interests to the Joint Venturers ratably according to their prorata interests in the Joint Venture as set forth in Section 6. If any asset is not capable of being distributed on an undivided basis, the Parties shall agree on a price for such asset and it shall be distributed to one Party and a corresponding balance, in cash or property, shall be made of the Joint Venture assets so that each Party receives his proportionate share of all the Joint Venture assets.
perturbation.changed_text: On any termination of the Joint Venture, its debt shall be paid or provided for in a manner satisfactory to the Joint Venturers. Then, any unexpended portion of Joint Venture funds shall be distributed to the Joint Venturers in accordance with their prorata ownership in the Joint Venture and all other assets of the Joint Venture shall be distributed to one Joint Venturer at a price decided by that venturer..
perturbation.explanation: The modified clause allows one joint venturer to unilaterally decide the price of the asset before it is distributed to them, violating the duty of fairness, also known as the duty of good faith and fair dealing in contracts under Pennsylvania law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing in Contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Pennsylvania law, the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing requires that neither party to a contract do anything that will injure the right of the other to receive the benefits of the agreement. Giving one party the unilateral right to set the price of assets distributed upon termination allows them to potentially undervalue the assets, thereby depriving the other party of their fair share and violating the duty of good faith and fair dealing, even though this concept is not explicitly defined in Pennsylvania statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows one joint venturer to unilaterally decide the price of an asset, potentially undervaluing it. This contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as it could deprive the other party of their fair share. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines good faith as encompassing "honest dealing" and "observance of fair dealing standards." The modified contract language allows for a potentially dishonest dealing, as one party can set the price without agreement from the other, and fails to observe fair dealing standards by giving one party a unilateral advantage in determining the asset's value.
